paper_title: High-Fidelity Relightable Monocular Portrait Animation with Lighting-Controllable Video Diffusion Model

core_story.problem: Obviously, the exact separation between intrinsic features and extrinsic features is crucial to reach our goal.
core_story.limitation: However, the ability of manipulating lighting during portrait anima- tion remains under-explored, which is highly important for seamlessly blending the generated foreground portrait with backgrounds under varying lighting conditions.
core_story.insight: Abstract Relightable portrait animation aims to animate a static ref- erence portrait to match the head movements of a driving video while adapting to user-specified or reference light- ing conditions.
core_story.proposed_method: With the I2V diffusion model, we propose a novel Lighting Controllable Video Diffusion model ( LCVD) to achieve high-fidelity, relightable portrait animation.
core_story.experimental_evidence: Extensive evaluations show that LCVD outperforms state-of-the-art methods in lighting re- alism, image quality, and video consistency, setting a new benchmark in relightable portrait animation.
core_story.summary: Obviously, the exact separation between intrinsic features and extrinsic features is crucial to reach our goal. However, the ability of manipulating lighting during portrait anima- tion remains under-explored, which is highly important for seamlessly blending the generated foreground portrait with backgrounds under varying lighting conditions. Abstract Relightable portrait animation aims to animate a static ref- erence portrait to match the head movements of a driving video while adapting to user-specified or reference light- ing conditions. With the I2V diffusion model, we propose a novel Lighting Controllable Video Diffusion model ( LCVD) to achieve high-fidelity, relightable portrait animation. Extensive evaluations show that LCVD outperforms state-of-the-art methods in lighting re- alism, image quality, and video consistency, setting a new benchmark in relightable portrait animation.

introduction_structure.main_message: Introduction Portrait animation has wide applications in video confer- encing, virtual reality, and the film industry.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Contrasts prior work and emphasizes unresolved weaknesses.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: Introduction Portrait animation has wide applications in video confer- encing, virtual reality, and the film industry. With the rapid advancement of GANs [15, 24, 25] and Diffusion Models [18, 40, 46], existing portrait animation methods [11, 16, 50, 57, 59] have demonstrated remarkable capa- bilities in generating talking faces. For instance, the state- of-the-art LivePortrait [16] achieves real-time, high-fidelity portrait animation by designing better motion transforma- tion and scaling up the talking head datasets. However, the ability of manipulating lighting during portrait anima- tion remains under-explored, which is highly important for seamlessly blending the generated foreground portrait with backgrounds under varying lighting conditions.

introduction_structure.main_message: In this paper, we focus on relightable portrait anima- tion.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Transitions from problem framing to the high-level solution.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: In this paper, we focus on relightable portrait anima- tion. We aim to animate a portrait in a still reference im- age, ensuring that the animated portrait’s movements match those in the driving video and its lighting aligns with the user-specified conditions. From the perspective of face attributes, we reduce the task to preserving the intrinsic features (identity and appearance) of the reference portrait while effectively transferring the extrinsic ones (given pose and lighting) to the reference portrait. Obviously, the exact separation between intrinsic features and extrinsic features is crucial to reach our goal.

introduction_structure.main_message: A key reason existing portrait animation methods cannot independently manipulate light- ing is their inability to decompose these two types of fea- tures.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Builds context and advances the paper's argument.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: A key reason existing portrait animation methods cannot independently manipulate light- ing is their inability to decompose these two types of fea- tures. In order to achieve high-fidelity relightable portrait an- imation, our key idea is to distinguish these two types of features by learning their feature subspaces, then maintain the intrinsic facial features and transfer external features. Observing that the image-to-video (I2V) diffusion model [5], trained on a large-scale dataset encompassing a variety of portraits with different lighting, poses, identities and ap- pearances provides a foundation for learning the two feature subspaces, we design two adapters to separately model the extrinsic and intrinsic feature subspaces within the feature space of I2V . Specifically, we represent the portrait’s extrin- sic attributes using shading hints rendered with the refer- ence image’s 3D mesh, target lighting, and driving frame’s pose, while the intrinsic attributes are represented by the reference image.

introduction_structure.main_message: During the self-supervised training pro- cess, we design a shading adapter to map the shading hints into an extrinsic feature subspace and a reference adapter to map the reference image into an intrinsic feature sub- space.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Transitions from problem framing to the high-level solution.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: During the self-supervised training pro- cess, we design a shading adapter to map the shading hints into an extrinsic feature subspace and a reference adapter to map the reference image into an intrinsic feature sub- space. By merging the features from these two subspaces, the model generates portraits with specified lighting, pose, identity, and appearance. With the I2V diffusion model, we propose a novel Lighting Controllable Video Diffusion model ( LCVD) to achieve high-fidelity, relightable portrait animation. First, we use an off-the-shelf model [14] to extract the 3D mesh, pose, and spherical harmonics lighting coefficients for each frame of the target video.

introduction_structure.main_message: Then an image is rendered as a guidance image, referred to as a shading hint, which en- codes lighting and pose information.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Builds context and advances the paper's argument.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: Then an image is rendered as a guidance image, referred to as a shading hint, which en- codes lighting and pose information. In the training stage, to enable pose alignment and lighting control, we use a shad- ing adapter to map the shading hints to the extrinsic feature subspace, representing external portrait attributes by estab- lishing a mapping between the shading hints and the target portrait. For identity and appearance preservation, we use a reference adapter to map the reference image to the intrinsic feature subspace, representing internal portrait attributes by creating a mapping between an initial frame and subsequent frames. Finally, during the inference stage, we merge the features from the two subspaces and input them into the I2V diffusion model to generate portraits with specified lighting, pose, identity, and appearance.

introduction_structure.main_message: To further control the light- ing magnitude, we employ multi-condition classifier-free guidance to emphasize the influence of the shading adapter and reduce the reference’s impact on relighting.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Enumerates takeaways to make the paper's value explicit.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: To further control the light- ing magnitude, we employ multi-condition classifier-free guidance to emphasize the influence of the shading adapter and reduce the reference’s impact on relighting. Our main contributions are listed as follows: • We introduce a Lighting Controllable Video Diffusion model, which overcomes the limitations of current por- trait animation methods that fail to manipulate lighting while animating the portrait. • We propose a shading adapter and a reference adapter to construct the feature subspaces for extrinsic and intrin- sic facial features, respectively. By merging these two subspaces, the I2V model is guided to achieve relightable portrait animation. • Extensive experiments demonstrate that LCVD surpasses state-of-the-art methods, showing significant improve- ments in metrics measuring lighting effects, image qual- ity, and video consistency.

method_structure.purpose: Introduces part of the proposed approach.
method_structure.author_introduction_strategy: Presents the method incrementally from overview to components.

experiment_structure.purpose: Presents experimental evidence supporting the paper.
experiment_structure.author_introduction_strategy: Uses staged evidence from setup to comparison and analysis.

Our extensive experimental results demonstrate that LCVD significantly outperforms existing methods in light- ing realism, image quality, and video consistency.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
Relightable portrait animation aims to animate a static ref-
erence portrait to match the head movements of a driving
video while adapting to user-specified or reference light-
ing conditions. Existing portrait animation methods fail
to achieve relightable portraits because they do not sep-
arate and manipulate intrinsic (identity and appearance)
†Corresponding author: Yanli Liu.
and extrinsic (pose and lighting) features. In this paper,
we present a Lighting Controllable Video Diffusion model
(LCVD) for high-fidelity, relightable portrait animation. We
address this limitation by distinguishing these feature types
through dedicated subspaces within the feature space of a
pre-trained image-to-video diffusion model. Specifically,
for each frame, we use the 3D mesh of the reference por-
trait, the pose of the driving frame as well as the speci-
fied lighting to render an image called shading hint. While
This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;
the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.
228

the reference image represents the intrinsic attributes, the
shading hint encodes the extrinsic attributes. In the train-
ing phase, we employ a reference adapter to map the ref-
erence into an intrinsic feature subspace and a shading
adapter to map the shading hints into an extrinsic fea-
ture subspace. By merging features from these subspaces,
the model achieves nuanced control over lighting and pose
in generated animations. Extensive evaluations show that
LCVD outperforms state-of-the-art methods in lighting re-
alism, image quality, and video consistency, setting a new
benchmark in relightable portrait animation. Our project
is available at https://github.com/MingtaoGuo/Relightable-
Portrait-Animation
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
Portrait animation has wide applications in video confer-
encing, virtual reality, and the film industry. With the
rapid advancement of GANs [15, 24, 25] and Diffusion
Models [18, 40, 46], existing portrait animation methods
[11, 16, 50, 57, 59] have demonstrated remarkable capa-
bilities in generating talking faces. For instance, the state-
of-the-art LivePortrait [16] achieves real-time, high-fidelity
portrait animation by designing better motion transforma-
tion and scaling up the talking head datasets. However,
the ability of manipulating lighting during portrait anima-
tion remains under-explored, which is highly important for
seamlessly blending the generated foreground portrait with
backgrounds under varying lighting conditions.
In this paper, we focus on relightable portrait anima-
tion. We aim to animate a portrait in a still reference im-
age, ensuring that the animated portrait’s movements match
those in the driving video and its lighting aligns with the
user-specified conditions. From the perspective of face
attributes, we reduce the task to preserving the intrinsic
features (identity and appearance) of the reference portrait
while effectively transferring the extrinsic ones (given pose
and lighting) to the reference portrait. Obviously, the exact
separation between intrinsic features and extrinsic features
is crucial to reach our goal. A key reason existing portrait
animation methods cannot independently manipulate light-
ing is their inability to decompose these two types of fea-
tures.
In order to achieve high-fidelity relightable portrait an-
imation, our key idea is to distinguish these two types of
features by learning their feature subspaces, then maintain
the intrinsic facial features and transfer external features.
Observing that the image-to-video (I2V) diffusion model
[5], trained on a large-scale dataset encompassing a variety
of portraits with different lighting, poses, identities and ap-
pearances provides a foundation for learning the two feature
subspaces, we design two adapters to separately model the
extrinsic and intrinsic feature subspaces within the feature
space of I2V . Specifically, we represent the portrait’s extrin-
sic attributes using shading hints rendered with the refer-
ence image’s 3D mesh, target lighting, and driving frame’s
pose, while the intrinsic attributes are represented by the
reference image. During the self-supervised training pro-
cess, we design a shading adapter to map the shading hints
into an extrinsic feature subspace and a reference adapter
to map the reference image into an intrinsic feature sub-
space. By merging the features from these two subspaces,
the model generates portraits with specified lighting, pose,
identity, and appearance.
With the I2V diffusion model, we propose a novel
Lighting Controllable Video Diffusion model ( LCVD) to
achieve high-fidelity, relightable portrait animation. First,
we use an off-the-shelf model [14] to extract the 3D mesh,
pose, and spherical harmonics lighting coefficients for each
frame of the target video. Then an image is rendered as
a guidance image, referred to as a shading hint, which en-
codes lighting and pose information. In the training stage, to
enable pose alignment and lighting control, we use a shad-
ing adapter to map the shading hints to the extrinsic feature
subspace, representing external portrait attributes by estab-
lishing a mapping between the shading hints and the target
portrait. For identity and appearance preservation, we use a
reference adapter to map the reference image to the intrinsic
feature subspace, representing internal portrait attributes by
creating a mapping between an initial frame and subsequent
frames. Finally, during the inference stage, we merge the
features from the two subspaces and input them into the I2V
diffusion model to generate portraits with specified lighting,
pose, identity, and appearance. To further control the light-
ing magnitude, we employ multi-condition classifier-free
guidance to emphasize the influence of the shading adapter
and reduce the reference’s impact on relighting.
Our main contributions are listed as follows:
• We introduce a Lighting Controllable Video Diffusion
model, which overcomes the limitations of current por-
trait animation methods that fail to manipulate lighting
while animating the portrait.
• We propose a shading adapter and a reference adapter to
construct the feature subspaces for extrinsic and intrin-
sic facial features, respectively. By merging these two
subspaces, the I2V model is guided to achieve relightable
portrait animation.
• Extensive experiments demonstrate that LCVD surpasses
state-of-the-art methods, showing significant improve-
ments in metrics measuring lighting effects, image qual-
ity, and video consistency.
raw_sections.conclusion:
6. Conclusion
In this paper, we introduce a Lighting Controllable Video
Diffusion model (LCVD) for high-fidelity, relightable por-
trait animation. By distinguishing between intrinsic and ex-
trinsic facial features, the model effectively preserves iden-
tity and appearance while enabling precise control over
lighting and pose. The model also incorporates multi-
condition classifier-free guidance to fine-tune the lighting
effects. Our extensive experimental results demonstrate that
LCVD significantly outperforms existing methods in light-
ing realism, image quality, and video consistency.
